schools.summary: Gill: the LORD called unto Moses, or met him, speaking to him out of the tabernacle, now that it was set up and the glory of the LORD filled it; the witnesses note God calling Moses and speaking to him out of the tabernacle, to give the law of the offerings — the opening of the book of sacrifices, God speaking from the dwelling he had filled with his glory (the tabernacle reared, God now speaking from it: 'the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation'; the calling, 'the LORD called unto Moses,' a gracious calling, that he might draw near to receive God's word; the place, 'out of the tabernacle of the congregation,' that God now spoke, not from Sinai, but from the tabernacle, the place of meeting he had just filled with his glory; the connexion, that, the dwelling being reared and the glory come, God begins to give from it the laws of the offerings, by which his people might draw near and have communion with him; the meaning the commentators draw, that God, having come to dwell among his people, teaches them the way of acceptable approach, for sinful men cannot come to the holy God but by sacrifice; that the way to God is opened by his own appointment).

themes: And the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation
saying
the LORD calling Moses and speaking from the tabernacle now reared and filled with his glory
God teaching from his dwelling the way of acceptable approach by sacrifice, for sinful men cannot come to the holy God but by it

schools.summary: Gill: the law of sacrifices was given to the children of Israel, not to the Gentiles, and the offering was to be brought of the cattle, of the herd and of the flock; the witnesses note God charging Israel that, if any man would bring an offering to the LORD, it should be of the cattle, the herd or the flock — the appointing of the kinds of offering and the freeness of the bringing (the offering of the clean herd and flock, brought by him who will: 'If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD, ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock'; the persons, 'the children of Israel,' that the law of sacrifices was given to God's covenant people, not to the Gentiles; the freeness, 'if any man of you bring an offering,' that the bringing was, in this kind, free and voluntary, of him whose heart moved him; the matter, 'of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock,' that the offering should be of the clean domestic animals, the ox of the herd or the sheep or goat of the flock, such as men possessed and lived by; the meaning the commentators draw, that God appointed for sacrifice the clean and useful creatures, which men must give of their own substance, at cost to themselves; the type, that these clean victims shadow Christ, the one true offering, of whom all the sacrifices spoke).

themes: If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD
ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock
God appointing the offering of the clean herd or flock, brought freely by him who would
the clean victims, given of men's own substance, shadowing Christ, the one true offering of whom all the sacrifices spoke

schools.summary: Gill: the burnt sacrifice was so called because consumed by fire, all of it except the skin, offered at the door of the tabernacle; the witnesses note God appointing the burnt sacrifice of the herd to be a male without blemish, offered of the man's own voluntary will at the door before the LORD — the burnt offering, wholly consumed, unblemished, and willingly brought (the victim perfect and the offering free: 'If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish... of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle'; the kind, 'a burnt sacrifice,' so called because consumed by fire, all of it save the skin burnt to God, the type of entire devotion; the victim, 'a male without blemish,' a perfect, unblemished male, the best and strongest, a type of Christ, the Lamb without blemish and without spot; the freeness, 'of his own voluntary will,' that it was offered willingly, a type of Christ, who offered himself freely, and of the believer's free devotion; the place, 'at the door of the tabernacle... before the LORD,' the one appointed place of meeting and acceptance; the type, that the whole burnt offering shadows Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweet savour, and the believer's whole consecration).

debates.question: What does the burnt offering — wholly consumed, unblemished, willingly offered — typify?

sides.position: Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweet savour — the male without blemish a type of the Lamb without spot (1 Peter 1:19), offered of his own voluntary will (Christ who offered himself freely, Hebrews 9:14); and the believer's whole consecration (Romans 12:1)

themes: If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish
he shall offer it of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD
the burnt offering wholly consumed, a male without blemish, willingly offered at the door
a type of Christ's entire offering of himself, the Lamb without spot offered freely, and the believer's whole consecration

schools.summary: Gill: he was to put his hand, according to the Targum his right hand, on the head of the burnt offering, transferring as it were his guilt and confessing his sins over it; the witnesses note the offerer putting his hand on the head of the burnt offering, that it should be accepted for him to make atonement — the laying on of hands, the substitution of the victim, the great gospel in the offering (by the hand laid on, the sin transferred and the victim accepted in his stead: 'he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering; and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him'; the act, 'put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering,' that the offerer laid his hand, the right hand, on the victim's head, transferring as it were his guilt and confessing his sin over it, the victim standing in his stead; the acceptance, 'it shall be accepted for him,' that the offering was accepted in the offerer's room, he accepted in and through the sacrifice; the end, 'to make atonement for him,' that even the burnt offering, wholly devoted, made atonement, the blood covering sin; the great type the commentators draw, that the laying on of hands shadows the transfer of guilt to the substitute, fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD hath laid the iniquity of us all, and the believer's acceptance in the Beloved; that we are accepted not in ourselves but in our Sacrifice).

debates.question: What does laying the hand on the head of the offering, and its being 'accepted for him,' signify?

sides.position: The transfer of guilt to the substitute and acceptance in its stead — fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all (Isaiah 53:6); the believer accepted not in himself but in his Sacrifice, accepted 'in the Beloved' (Ephesians 1:6)

themes: he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering
and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him
the offerer transferring his guilt to the victim, accepted in his stead to make atonement
the great type of the transfer of guilt to the substitute, fulfilled in Christ, and the believer's acceptance in the Beloved

schools.summary: Gill: the man that brought the burnt offering was to kill the bullock, and the priests, Aaron's sons, were to take the blood and sprinkle it round about upon the altar; the witnesses note the offerer killing the bullock before the LORD, and the priests bringing the blood and sprinkling it round about the altar — the death of the victim and the application of the atoning blood (the substitute slain and his blood put upon the altar: 'he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests... shall bring the blood, and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar'; the slaying, 'he shall kill the bullock before the LORD,' that the offerer himself slew the victim, the wages of sin being death, the victim dying in his stead; the priests' office, 'the priests, Aaron's sons, shall bring the blood,' that the priests received the blood and brought it to the altar, the work of the priest beginning with the blood; the sprinkling, 'sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar,' that the blood, the life, was cast all about the altar, presented to God for atonement; the great type the commentators draw, that the slaying and the sprinkled blood shadow the death of Christ and the application of his blood, without shedding of blood is no remission, the blood of sprinkling that speaks better things; that atonement is made by the blood of the substitute).

themes: he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall bring the blood
and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
the victim slain in the offerer's stead, its blood sprinkled round the altar for atonement
the great type of the death of Christ and the application of his blood; without shedding of blood is no remission

schools.summary: Gill: he was to flay the burnt offering, take off its skin, which was the only part not burnt and was the property of the priest, and to cut it into pieces; the witnesses note the offerer flaying the burnt offering and cutting it into its pieces — the preparing of the victim for the altar, laid open and divided (the whole opened and parted for the fire: 'he shall flay the burnt offering, and cut it into his pieces'; the flaying, 'he shall flay the burnt offering,' that the skin was taken off, the only part not burnt, which became the property of the priest; the cutting, 'cut it into his pieces,' that the body was divided into its parts, the better to be laid on the altar and consumed; the meaning some draw, that the flaying and cutting open of the victim, all laid bare, may shadow the laying open of Christ in his sufferings, nothing hidden, and the searching of the inward parts; that the whole victim, opened and divided, was made ready to be wholly offered to God; that nothing of the burnt offering was kept back, all save the skin given to the fire).

themes: And he shall flay the burnt offering
and cut it into his pieces
the burnt offering flayed and cut into pieces, the whole opened and made ready for the fire
some seeing the laying open of the victim shadow the laying bare of Christ in his sufferings; nothing kept back, all given to the fire

schools.summary: Gill: the fire of the altar originally came down from heaven and consumed the sacrifice, a token of God's acceptance, and this fire was kept burning continually; the witnesses note the priests putting fire upon the altar and laying the wood in order upon it — the kindling of the altar-fire and the laying of the wood for the burning (the fire set and the wood ordered for the sacrifice: 'the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar, and lay the wood in order upon the fire'; the fire, 'put fire upon the altar,' that the altar-fire originally came down from heaven and consumed the sacrifice, a token of God's acceptance, and was thereafter kept burning continually, never to go out; the wood, 'lay the wood in order upon the fire,' that the wood was laid in order, the fuel for the burning; the meaning some draw, that the fire, of heavenly origin and ever burning, may signify the holiness and justice of God, that consumed the offering, and the fervour of the Spirit; that the sacrifice was consumed by a fire of God's own kindling, the token of his acceptance; the type, that Christ's offering was consumed by the fire of divine justice, yet accepted of God).

themes: And the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar
and lay the wood in order upon the fire
the priests setting the altar-fire (which came from heaven and burned continually) and laying the wood
some seeing the fire signify the holiness and justice of God; Christ's offering consumed by divine justice, yet accepted of God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire upon the altar; the witnesses note the priests laying the parts of the victim, the head and the fat, in order on the wood that was on the fire — the orderly laying of the divided victim upon the altar-fire (every part laid in order for the burning: 'the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire'; the office, 'the priests... shall lay the parts,' that the priests, having received the parts, laid them on the altar; the parts, 'the head, and the fat,' with the rest of the pieces, each laid in order upon the wood; the orderliness, 'in order,' that all was done decently and in order, every part set in its place upon the fire; the meaning some draw, that the whole victim, laid piece by piece on the fire, signifies the entire devotion of the offering, nothing kept back, the head, the understanding, and the fat, the best and richest, all given to God; that what is offered to God is to be offered wholly and in order; the type, that Christ offered himself wholly to God, every part devoted).

themes: And the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat
in order upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar
the priests laying the parts, the head and the fat, in order on the altar-fire, every part in its place
some seeing the whole victim laid piece by piece signify entire devotion, nothing kept back, all given wholly and in order

schools.summary: Gill: the inwards and legs were washed in water, done first in a room in the court called the washing room, where they washed the inwards of the holy things; the witnesses note the inwards and legs washed in water, and the priest burning all on the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour to the LORD — the washing of the inner parts and the wholly burning of the offering, well-pleasing to God (the whole, made clean, burnt to God for a sweet savour: 'his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar... an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the washing, 'his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water,' that the inner parts and legs were washed clean, in the washing room, that nothing unclean should be offered; the burning, 'the priest shall burn all on the altar,' that the whole victim, save the skin, was wholly consumed to God, the mark of the burnt offering; the acceptance, 'an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that the burning was a sweet savour, well-pleasing and accepted of God; the meaning the commentators draw, that the washing of the inwards signifies the purity within that God requires, and the whole burning the entire devotion of the offering; the great type before given, that the whole burnt offering, washed and wholly consumed, a sweet savour, shadows Christ's offering of himself, a sweetsmelling savour, pure within and wholly devoted).

debates.question: What does the burnt offering, washed within and wholly burnt, a sweet savour, typify?

sides.position: Christ's offering of himself to God, 'a sweetsmelling savour' (Ephesians 5:2) — pure within (the washed inwards signifying the purity God requires) and wholly devoted (the whole burning); and the entire consecration of the believer

themes: his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar
an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD
the inwards washed clean and the whole burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD
the washing signifying purity within, the whole burning entire devotion; a type of Christ's offering, 'a sweetsmelling savour'

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here are the laws concerning the burnt-offerings of the flock, for those of the middle rank, who could not well afford to offer a bullock, would bring a sheep or a goat; the witnesses note God appointing the burnt offering of the flock, of the sheep or goats, to be a male without blemish — the lesser offering for the middle sort, the same in kind though smaller (the man of less means bringing a sheep or goat: 'if his offering be of the flocks... of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice; he shall bring it a male without blemish'; the kind, 'of the flocks... of the sheep, or of the goats,' a smaller victim than the bullock, for those of the middle rank, who could not well afford to offer a bullock; the same requirement, 'a male without blemish,' that, though smaller, it must still be a perfect, unblemished male, the same in kind and quality, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; the gracious provision the commentators draw, that God made room for the offering of every man according to his ability, the middle sort not shut out, but each accepted in his own measure; that God accepts the offering according to what a man hath; that the same Christ is offered, whether the offerer be rich or of middle estate).

themes: if his offering be of the flocks, namely, of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice
he shall bring it a male without blemish
the burnt offering of the flock, a perfect male of the sheep or goats, for those of middle rank
God making room for the offering of every man according to his ability, the same Christ offered whether rich or of middle estate

schools.summary: Gill: this is a circumstance not mentioned in the killing of the bullock; Maimonides says there was a square place from the wall of the altar northward where the offerings were slain; the witnesses note the victim of the flock killed on the north side of the altar before the LORD, and the priests sprinkling its blood round about — the slaying in the appointed place and the application of the blood (the flock-offering too slain and its blood put on the altar: 'he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD: and the priests... shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar'; the place, 'on the side of the altar northward,' that the flock-victim was slain on the north side, in the appointed square place for killing the offerings, a circumstance here named; the slaying, 'before the LORD,' in God's presence, the victim dying in the offerer's stead; the blood, 'the priests... shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar,' that, as with the bullock, the blood was cast round the altar for atonement; that the same order was kept for the flock as for the herd, the blood ever the ground of atonement; the type before given, that the slaying and the sprinkled blood shadow the death of Christ and the application of his blood; that there is one way of atonement, by the blood of the substitute).

themes: he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD
and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar
the flock-victim slain on the north side and its blood sprinkled round about for atonement
the same order as for the herd, the blood ever the ground of atonement; a type of the death of Christ and his blood

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'cut it into his pieces,' in the same manner as the bullock, that the parts might be laid in order upon the altar; the witnesses note the flock-victim cut into its pieces, with the head and fat, and the priest laying them in order on the wood and fire — the dividing and orderly laying of the flock-offering, as with the herd (the lesser victim too cut and laid in order: 'he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat: and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire'; the cutting, 'cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat,' that the victim was divided in the same manner as the bullock, head, fat, and the rest; the laying, 'the priest shall lay them in order on the wood,' that the parts were laid in order on the altar-fire, as before; that the same order was kept for the flock as for the herd, every part laid in order for the burning; the meaning before given, that the whole victim, divided and laid in order, signifies the entire devotion of the offering, nothing kept back; that what is offered to God is offered wholly and in order, whether the offering be great or small; the type, that Christ offered himself wholly to God).

themes: And he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat
and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar
the flock-victim cut into pieces and laid in order on the altar-fire, the same order as for the herd
the whole divided and laid in order signifying entire devotion, what is offered to God offered wholly and in order, great or small

schools.summary: Gill: the inwards and legs were washed with water, as was done with the bullock, before the whole was offered upon the altar; the witnesses note the inwards and legs of the flock-victim washed, the priest bringing and offering all on the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour to the LORD — the washing and wholly offering of the flock-offering, as with the herd, well-pleasing to God (the lesser offering too, washed, wholly burnt for a sweet savour: 'he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the washing, 'wash the inwards and the legs with water,' that the inner parts were washed clean, as was done with the bullock, that nothing unclean be offered; the offering, 'the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar,' that the whole was offered, wholly burnt to God; the acceptance, 'it is a burnt sacrifice... of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that this offering too was a sweet savour, well-pleasing and accepted; the great lesson the commentators draw, that the offering of the middle sort, though smaller, was as fully accepted, of a sweet savour, as the costlier, God accepting according to what a man hath; that the same sweet savour rose from the lesser offering as from the greater, the same Christ being offered; that God accepts the willing and pure offering, great or small).

themes: he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar
it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD
the flock-victim washed and wholly offered, a sweet savour to the LORD
the lesser offering as fully accepted as the costlier; the same sweet savour, the same Christ offered, God accepting great or small

schools.summary: Gill: the offering of fowls was for the poorer sort, who could not offer a bullock, nor a sheep, nor a lamb; Charles Ellicott: the fowls here are in contrast to the cattle, a lesser offering for those of less means; the witnesses note God appointing the burnt sacrifice of fowls, turtledoves or young pigeons, for him who could bring no larger offering — the gracious provision for the poorest, that even the poor might bring an acceptable offering (God making room for the poor man's offering: 'if the burnt sacrifice... be of fowls, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons'; the kind, 'of fowls... turtledoves, or... young pigeons,' the smallest and cheapest of offerings, for the poorer sort, who could not offer a bullock, nor a sheep, or a lamb; the contrast Ellicott marks, that the fowls here are in contrast to the cattle, a lesser offering for those of less means; the gracious provision the commentators draw, that God so ordered the law that even the poorest, who had but a pair of doves, might bring an offering and be accepted, none shut out for poverty; that God accepts the offering of the poor as truly as that of the rich, according to what a man hath; the type, that the same Christ was offered in the poor man's dove as in the rich man's bullock, and that Christ himself, the poor man's Lord, was redeemed with a pair of doves).

debates.question: What does the provision of fowls for the burnt offering teach?

sides.position: God's gracious provision for the poorest — that even the poor, who could not afford a bullock or a sheep, might bring a pair of doves and be accepted, none shut out for poverty; God accepting according to what a man hath, the same Christ offered in the poor man's dove as in the rich man's bullock (and Christ himself redeemed with a pair of doves, Luke 2:24)

themes: if the burnt sacrifice... be of fowls, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons
the gracious provision for the poorest
the burnt sacrifice of fowls for the poorest, who could not afford a bullock or a sheep
God's provision that even the poor might bring an acceptable offering, the same Christ offered, none shut out for poverty

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off its head, and burn it on the altar, and the blood shall be wrung out at the side of the altar; the witnesses note the priest bringing the bird to the altar, wringing off its head, burning it, and wringing out its blood at the side — the offering of the poor man's bird, slain and its blood applied, as with the greater victims (the bird too slain and its blood put to the altar: 'the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar; and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar'; the bringing, 'the priest shall bring it unto the altar,' to the southeast horn, near the place of the ashes; the slaying, 'wring off his head,' that, the bird being small, the priest killed it by wringing or pinching off the head; the blood, 'the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar,' that the little blood of the bird was pressed out against the altar's side, as the blood of the greater victims was sprinkled; that the same atoning use was made of the poor man's offering, his bird slain and its blood applied; the meaning some draw, that the death and the blood of the small offering had the same atoning value as the great, the same Christ being shadowed; that the poor man's offering was as truly a sacrifice as the rich man's).

themes: the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar
and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar
the priest wringing off the bird's head and pressing out its blood against the altar's side
the same atoning use made of the poor man's offering as of the greater, the same Christ shadowed, as truly a sacrifice

schools.summary: Gill: he was to pluck away its crop with its feathers, or with what was in its crop, as Onkelos renders it, the dung, and cast it beside the altar on the east, by the place of the ashes; the witnesses note the priest plucking away the crop with its feathers and casting it beside the altar by the place of the ashes — the removing of the unclean part of the bird before the offering (what is uncomely put away: 'he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers, and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes'; the removing, 'pluck away his crop with his feathers,' that the crop, with what was in its crop, the refuse or dung, and the feathers, were plucked away, the unclean and uncomely parts not fit for the altar; the casting, 'cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes,' that these were cast aside, into the place where the ashes were laid; the meaning some draw, that, as the inwards of the greater victims were washed, so here the foul part of the bird was removed, that nothing unclean be offered, the offering made pure; that God will have his offerings clean, the refuse cast away; that even in the poor man's small offering, purity was required).

themes: And he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers
and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes
the crop with its refuse and the feathers plucked away and cast aside before the offering
that nothing unclean be offered; God having his offerings clean, purity required even in the poor man's small offering

schools.summary: Gill: he was to cleave it with the wings, one wing on one side and the other on the other, but not divide it asunder, the body though cleaved down the middle not parted, nor the wings separated; the witnesses note the priest cleaving the bird by its wings, yet not dividing it asunder, and burning it on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the offering of the poor man's bird, whole and undivided, wholly burnt and well-pleasing (even the small offering whole and a sweet savour: 'he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the cleaving, 'cleave it with the wings thereof,' that the body was cleaved down the middle, the wings left on either side; the limit, 'shall not divide it asunder,' that, unlike the greater victims cut into pieces, the small bird was not parted asunder, but left whole; the burning, 'the priest shall burn it upon the altar,' wholly consumed; the acceptance, 'a burnt sacrifice... of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that the poor man's bird, wholly burnt, was as truly a sweet savour as the rich man's bullock; the great lesson the commentators draw, that God accepts the poor man's offering as fully and sweetly as the rich man's, all according to what a man hath, the same Christ shadowed in great and small; that the willing and pure offering, though the least, is a sweet savour to God).

debates.question: What does the poor man's bird, wholly burnt and 'a sweet savour,' teach?

sides.position: That God accepts the poor man's offering as fully and sweetly as the rich man's — the small bird as truly 'a sweet savour' as the bullock — all according to what a man hath; the willing and pure offering, though the least, well-pleasing to God, the same Christ shadowed in great and small

themes: he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar
it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD
the poor man's bird cleaved yet not divided, wholly burnt, a sweet savour to the LORD
God accepting the poor man's offering as fully and sweetly as the rich man's, the same Christ shadowed in great and small
